Home › Static Gravity

Static Gravity

Static Gravity refers to the gravitational field produced by a mass distribution that is both stationary and non-rotating. In university-level General Relativity, a static spacetime is defined by the existence of a time-like Killing vector that is orthogonal to a family of space-like hypersurfaces. This represents the simplest possible state of a gravitational field, where there is no Frame-Dragging and no time-evolution, serving as the relativistic generalization of Newtonian Gravity.

1. Time-Translation Symmetry

A static field is invariant under time translations (\( t o t + \Delta t \)). This means that an observer at rest in the field will measure the same Metric Tensor at any moment. Mathematically, this requires all time-derivatives of the metric to be zero and the cross-terms between time and space (\( g_{ti} \)) to vanish. This "Pure" time-symmetry is what distinguishes static black holes (Schwarzschild) from stationary ones (Kerr).

2. The Gravitational Potential (Phi)

In the weak-field limit of static gravity, the metric component \( g_{00} \) is directly related to the Newtonian Potential: \( g_{00} \approx -(1 + 2\Phi/c^2) \). This provides the "Relativistic Bridge," showing that the "Force" of gravity we feel on Earth is just the gradient of the Gravitational Time Dilation. In a static field, clocks at different heights tick at different rates, a purely geometric effect of the mass-energy distribution.

3. Proper vs. Coordinate Distance

In static gravity, the "Distance" between two points is no longer a simple coordinate difference. Proper Distance is calculated by integrating the spatial part of the metric: \( dL = \sqrt{g_{rr}} dr \). Near a large mass, space is "Stretched," meaning the physical distance is greater than the coordinate change would suggest. This stretching is the source of the Shapiro Time Delay observed in signals passing near the Sun.

4. The Buchdahl Limit

Static gravity imposes a fundamental limit on the compactness of a star, known as the Buchdahl Limit. It states that for a stable, spherically symmetric fluid, the radius must be greater than \( 9/8 \) of the Schwarzschild Radius (\( R \gt 1.125 r_s \)). If a star is compressed beyond this static limit, no internal pressure can support it, and it must undergo Dynamical Collapse into a black hole.

5. Schwarzschild: The Static King

The Schwarzschild Metric is the unique vacuum solution for static gravity. It describes a world where gravity only "Pulls" and never "Twists." In this field, light follows Null Geodesics that bend toward the mass, and massive objects follow Timelike Geodesics (orbits). The static nature ensures that orbits are closed and stable, unlike the precessing orbits found in rotating spacetimes.

6. Breaking Stasis: Gravitational Waves

Static gravity is a "Zero-Radiation" state. According to General Relativity, a static field cannot emit Gravitational Waves. Waves only occur when the Quadrupole Moment of a system changes over time. Thus, the transition from "Static Gravity" to "Dynamic Gravity" marks the point where the begins to ripple, carrying energy and information across the universe at speed \( c \).

Key Theoretical Identities

Interact with identities to explore their semantic depth.

  • Stasis Rule [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ g_{ti} = 0 \quad ( ext{Orthogonality Condition}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Stasis Rule: The mathematical requirement that space and time do not "mix" in a non-rotating field.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Time Curvature [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ g_{00} \approx -(1 + \frac{2\Phi}{c^2}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Time Curvature: How the static gravitational field causes clocks to slow down as they approach a mass.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Spatial Curvature [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ R/M > 9/4 \quad ( ext{Buchdahl Stability}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Spatial Curvature: The physical stretching of space that makes distances near a mass longer than they appear.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Buchdahl Limit [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ \Delta au = \Delta t \sqrt{-g_{00}} \quad ( ext{Gravitational Redshift}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Buchdahl Limit: The maximum density any object can reach before being forced to become a black hole.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.
